narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put a lot of effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 and LSAT score of 22. Unfortunately, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated an 85.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those likely to pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile of 1, which is well below the typical student's midpoint ranking and signaled weaker academic performance in later undergraduate years. Your LSAT score of 22, far lower than the average of around 37, was the second-biggest driver, strongly indicating potential struggles with the rigorous reasoning needed for the bar. Even your Year 1 decile of 1, again much lower than average, added to this concern by highlighting early academic challenges. On a more positive note, your middle family income bracket nudged the prediction slightly toward success compared to lower incomes, and your undergraduate GPA, while below the 3.21 average, had a smaller pulling effect toward failure. Your choice of full-time study also offered a minor boost, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than typical admits in key academic deciles and standardized test scores, which overshadowed these positives. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework, or exploring part-time programs or alternative law schools with more flexible criteria. You have the determination to improve these areas, and many students turn predictions like this into success stories with targeted preparation. We're rooting for you to reapply stronger next time.
